Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty minimal in terms of structured planning. At 28, I've learned that I can absorb material quickly when I focus, so I didn't feel the need to spend hours on the LMS every week. I'd usually dedicate a few hours here and there, maybe 16 hours total in a week, but it was spread out unevenly. I relied heavily on my ability to cram effectively and understand concepts visually through diagrams and charts rather than grinding through every single resource.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. I have other responsibilities outside of university, so I couldn't always stick to a rigid schedule. When deadlines approached or exams were coming up, I'd buckle down and focus intensely. Otherwise, I'd let things slide a bit, knowing I could catch up when it mattered most.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just checking if there was anything new I needed to be aware of. I wasn't someone who logged in daily out of habit. If I knew there was a video I needed to watch or a discussion I should probably contribute to, that would prompt me. Sometimes boredom pushed me to check in, but that was rare.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my daily routine. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, especially early in the semester when the gamified tasks caught my attention briefly. But that novelty wore off quickly, and I went back to my sporadic usage pattern. It just felt like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my life.
Q5: The video content was actually quite useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a decent amount of video material because seeing concepts explained visually stuck with me better than reading text. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. But overall, I found that a targeted approach to resources worked better than trying to consume everything.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. They felt like busy work to me, and with my limited motivation, I couldn't justify spending time on things that weren't directly tied to my grade. The assignment completion stuff I struggled with because the assignments themselves felt tedious compared to just preparing for exams where I knew I could perform well.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam performance or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because they added some variety, but once I realized they didn't significantly impact my understanding or grades, my interest faded. I needed to see clear value, not just engagement for engagement's sake.
Q8: Optional activities felt exactly that—optional. I treated them as things I'd get to if I had spare time, which I rarely did. Required activities had a different weight because I knew they'd affect my grade. But even with required stuff, I was selective. If I could skip something and still do well on the exam, I probably would. It's not ideal, but that's how I operated.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and PDFs early in the semester and studied from those offline constantly. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks I found that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me visually. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with printed notes and diagrams spread out on my desk.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation looked like nothing in the LMS. I'd print materials, create my own visual summaries and mind maps, and study from those for hours. I also did practice problems from external sources that weren't tracked anywhere. The LMS would show I hadn't been active, but I was actually deep in study mode—just not in a way the system could see.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then never go back to the LMS for it. Why would I keep logging in when I already had the material saved locally? It was more efficient for me to work offline where I could organize things my way.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I felt completely disengaged and bored with the course. The middle of the semester was rough—the content felt repetitive, and my motivation was already low to begin with. Stress from other life responsibilities piled up too, making it hard to care about logging into the LMS when I had bigger things on my mind.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were a big factor. At 28, I'm juggling more than just coursework, and sometimes the LMS was the first thing to get deprioritized. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum online and rely on my exam skills to carry me through. Confusion with certain topics also made me avoid the LMS and seek explanations elsewhere.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, or I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. The boredom made it hard to stay present even when I was technically engaging with the platform. I was going through the motions more than actually learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. I knew they were important, but the motivation just wasn't there. The assignments felt like a chore compared to studying for exams, where I felt more confident in my abilities. I kept telling myself I'd catch up, but often I just... didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were the main thing that got me to actually do anything on the LMS. Without a deadline looming, I had no urgency to engage. When something was due, I'd suddenly become very active—logging in, submitting what I could, checking requirements. It was a very reactive approach rather than proactive, but it's how I functioned with low motivation.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but not as much as it probably should have. When I got positive feedback, it briefly boosted my engagement because I felt validated. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more because it felt discouraging. The gamified task feedback was interesting but didn't sustain my interest long-term.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit rigid for someone like me who prefers flexibility. Having set modules and expected participation timelines didn't match how I naturally learn. I would have engaged more if there was more freedom to go at my own pace. The structure made me feel behind even when I knew I could catch up for exams.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier—that matched my learning style. But the heavy emphasis on discussion posts and regular assignment submissions made it harder because those aren't my strengths. I felt like the course was designed for a different type of student, one who thrives on consistent small tasks rather than big exam performances.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring or trying to understand the platform without actually producing work. Or they could be like me—accessing materials to study offline but not completing the tracked assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just clicking around trying to figure out what's actually important.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity was low, but I scored 96 on my exam and got a Distinction. I studied effectively outside the system, used my visual learning strengths, and performed when it counted. The LMS just doesn't capture someone who learns efficiently through alternative methods and excels under exam pressure.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, which gives a surface-level picture of activity. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone actually understood the material. They definitely miss students like me who learn independently and only use the LMS as one small part of our study approach.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn it—not just going through the motions. For me, engagement happened mostly offline when I was creating visual summaries and really wrestling with concepts. It's more about mental investment than platform activity. You can be highly engaged without ever touching the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed notes, creating diagrams, or watching external videos that explained things better. It wouldn't show my exam preparation intensity or the fact that despite my boredom and low motivation, I still cared enough to perform well when it mattered. My real engagement was invisible to the system.
